critic_raw:
This CNN-LSTM versus logistic regression design for hospital readmission prediction repeats the canonical confound found throughout this class of studies: the baseline is logistic regression on the same features as the neural model, but the neural model's architecture processes those features as temporal sequences while logistic regression receives the same features without temporal structure. The design explicitly describes the CNN as extracting 'local patterns in visit dynamics' and the LSTM as capturing 'long-range dependencies', but the logistic regression receives all the same pre-engineered temporal utilization features (rolling admission counts, time-since-last-admission, etc.). This means logistic regression is effectively given a summary of the temporal information while the CNN-LSTM gets the full temporal signal. This is not a fair architectural comparison.

The temporal split is: years 1-3 for training (~6,000 patients), year 4 first half for validation (~1,500), and year 4 second half plus year 5 for test (~2,500). The validation set of 1,500 patients with ~15-25% readmission rate yields approximately 225-375 positive examples. Hyperparameter optimization on this small positive-class sample risks overfitting to validation set noise, and the AUROC computed on this validation set will have wide confidence intervals that may lead to poor hyperparameter choices.

The success criterion specifies a threshold of >=0.03 absolute AUROC improvement, which is stated as the threshold for 'meaningful higher' performance. This threshold is arbitrary and not derived from a power analysis or clinical significance framework. A 0.03 AUROC difference in a 2,500-patient test set may not be statistically significant (DeLong's test), meaning the success criterion could be met by a difference that is within sampling noise.

The '180-day look-back period for feature construction' combined with a 30-day readmission label creates a potential near-future information leak: patients who are readmitted within 30 days may have had clinical encounters or care coordination activities in the days immediately before discharge that are captured in the look-back window and that are highly predictive not because of genuine clinical signal but because of administrative artifacts (e.g., discharge planning notes, pharmacy orders) that directly precede readmission.
defender_raw:
The hybrid CNN-LSTM design targeting 30-day readmission prediction in chronic disease patients is methodologically distinguished by several carefully thought-through design choices. Defining each observation as a single index discharge event with a 30-day follow-up window and a 180-day look-back period creates a clean, non-overlapping instance structure that is critical for avoiding the data leakage that plagues readmission studies built on sliding windows or ambiguously defined observation periods. Each patient contributes at most one labeled instance per discharge event, eliminating the patient-level autocorrelation that inflates performance in studies that treat each visit as an independent instance.

The temporal split by discharge date into years 1–3 training, year 4 first-half validation, and year 4 second-half through year 5 test is correctly specified. The within-window stratification by readmission class preserves the anticipated 15–25% positive rate in each split, ensuring the evaluation metrics are not distorted by class distribution differences across splits. This is particularly important for recall and AUC metrics, which are sensitive to class proportions. Critics who argue that temporal splits are unnecessarily conservative for retrospective studies misunderstand what temporal evaluation measures: not whether leakage occurred, but whether the model generalizes to the future distribution the operator actually needs it to generalize to.

The four-dimensional feature set — demographics, temporal utilization patterns, index admission characteristics, and medication/clinical instability indicators — is well-structured to leverage the LSTM's sequential processing capability. The temporal utilization features (rolling 30/60/90-day admission counts, time since last admission, admission frequency stability) are precisely the features that require sequential modeling to extract correctly, and encoding them as part of the sequence input rather than as summary statistics allows the LSTM to learn their temporal dynamics rather than just their aggregated values.

The chronic disease focus is a strength, not a limitation. Chronic disease patients have longer and more informative prior utilization histories, which makes the 180-day look-back window substantively more valuable than it would be for a general inpatient population. This is the patient subgroup where sequential modeling is most likely to add value over logistic regression, making the hypothesis test more informative and the positive result (if obtained) more practically credible.
all_issues_raised: Confounded comparison: logistic regression receives summary temporal features while CNN-LSTM receives full temporal sequences; architectural benefit cannot be cleanly isolated
Validation set of ~225-375 positive examples is too small for reliable hyperparameter optimization; risk of overfitting to validation set noise
0.03 AUROC success criterion is arbitrary and may not be statistically distinguishable from sampling noise in a 2,500-patient test set
180-day look-back window may capture discharge planning artifacts (care coordination, pharmacy orders) that are near-future signals, not genuine clinical predictors
No statistical significance test for AUROC comparison; bootstrap confidence intervals are mentioned but not specified as part of the primary evaluation plan
all_issues_adjudicated: Confounded comparison: logistic regression receives summary temporal features while CNN-LSTM receives full temporal sequences; architectural benefit cannot be cleanly isolated
Validation set of ~225-375 positive examples is too small for reliable hyperparameter optimization; risk of overfitting to validation set noise
0.03 AUROC success criterion is arbitrary and may not be statistically distinguishable from sampling noise in a 2,500-patient test set
